LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANTS CIM URBAN PARTNERS, L.P., CIM URBAN REIT LLC., CIM MERGER SUB, LLC, CIM COMMERCIAL TRUST CORPORATION, SOUTHFORK MERGER SUB, LLC, CIM URBAN REIT 211 MAIN STREET SF LP AND CIM URBAN REIT PROPERTIES II LP'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT, AS TO BOTH CAUSES OF ACTION IN THE COMPLAINT IS OVERRULED AS TO BOTH CAUSES OF ACTION. DEFENDANTS FAIL TO IDENTIFY AN "ASSESSMENT" SCHEME UNDER THE SAN FRANCISCO ORDINANCE. ARTICLE 12-C SECTIONS 1102 AND 1115(A) PROVIDE THAT THE TRANSFER TAX IS A SELF-REPORTED TAX THAT IS DUE AND PAYABLE AT THE TIME OF THE PROPERTY TRANSFER. ALL DEADLINES IN THE CASE ARE STAYED FOR TEN DAYS. ORDER IS SIGNED. SEE MINUTES AND/OR TRANSCRIPT FOR DETAILS. JUDGE: HAROLD KAHN, CLERK: M. GOODMAN, COURT REPORTER: CAROL KAREN, CSR	711, CKAREN@SFTC.ORG

calendar.matters:
CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Nov-17-2017 Off Calendar

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Aug-21-2017 Continued To Dec-06-17 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-17-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Natalie Orr, Deputy City Attorney and Carole Ruwart Deputy City Attorney (415-554-3849) from the Office of the City Attorney for the Plaintiff.
Badley Marsh, Esq. (415-655-1252) and Michael Hogue, Esq. (415-665-1303) of Greenberg Traurig for Defendant.

RULING - ARGUED. The Court shares his concerns re: transfer tax ordinance. Defendant argues bad drafting, bad consequence, "lien" remedy, "collection" remedy, section 1115(D)notice provision. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts th tenative ruling with modification as follows:

DEFENDANTS CIM URBAN PARTNERS, L.P., CIM URBAN REIT LLC., CIM MERGER SUB, LLC, CIM COMMERCIAL TRUST CORPORATION, SOUTHFORK MERGER SUB, LLC, CIM URBAN REIT 211 MAIN STREET SF LP and CIM URBAN REIT PROPERTIES II LP'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT, as to both causes of action in the complaint is OVERRULED as to both causes of action. Defendants fail to identify an "assessment" scheme under the San Francisco ordinance. Article 12-C sections 1102 and 1115(a) provide that the transfer tax is a self-reported tax that is due and payable at the time of the property transfer.

Plaintiff City and County of San Francisco's ability to obtain a lien against the property does not displace its right to bring a judicial action to collect a tax. Defendants' position is antithetical to the "pay first, litigate later" rule of the California Constitution. Article 12-C section 1113 sets forth the post-payment administrative process to obtain a tax refund. It is doubtful whether the doctrine of exhaustion of administrative remedies even applies to CCSF. The policies underlying the exhaustion doctrine include limiting judicial intervention in the executive decision-making process; affording agencies an opportunity to review and correct their own errors; deference to administrative expertise; and promoting judicial efficiency. (Farmers Ins. Exchange v. Sup. Crt (1992) 2 Cal.4th 377, 391; Williams & Fickett v. County of Fresno (2017) 2 Cal.5th 1258, 1268.) Where the party seeking judicial intervention is a governmental body, the doctrine's applicability does not further, or only minimally serves, these policies. City of Oakland v. Hotels.com LP (2009) 572 F.3d 958, 962 is not binding authority and is distinguishable because it examined a different taxing administrative process. At any rate, the court need not resolve the exhaustion issue because defendants mischaracterize the San Francisco ordinance. Defendants' other arguments also lack merit. Defendants fail to show that CCSF's purported failure to send notice to each individual defendant defeats this action. Paragraphs 30 and 39 of the complaint indicate that defendants share a common manager and the court can infer actual notice. Defendants' arguments about notice and the meaning of Proposition W are substantive arguments that should be raised in an Art 12-C section 1113 hearing. (Article 12-C section 1113.3(b) ["[N]o claim or defense that, for any reason, a tax is not due or cannot be applied under this code may be raised in any judicial proceeding except in an action for refund of the disputed tax."]. Defendants' reference to San Francisco Administrative Code section 10.23 is irrelevant. CCSF correctly note that this provision has nothing to do with defendants. It is a provision regarding interdepartmental communication.

All deadlines in the case are stayed for ten days. Order is signed. Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR 711, ckaren@sftc.org =(302/HK)
